cards.back: A concept in game theory where no player can improve their payoff (result) by unilaterally changing their strategy, assuming all other players keep their strategies unchanged.

cards.front: Who introduced the concept of Nash equilibrium?
cards.back: John Nash, an American mathematician and economist, introduced the concept in 1950 through his work on the Minimax Theorem.

cards.front: What is the primary assumption for a Nash equilibrium?
cards.back: Rationality: all players are assumed to be rational and make decisions based on their own self-interest.

cards.front: What is a characteristic of a Nash equilibrium?
cards.back: No player can improve their payoff by changing their strategy, assuming all other players keep their strategies unchanged.

cards.front: What type of game can a Nash equilibrium be found in?
cards.back: Both cooperative and non-cooperative games, including zero-sum and non-zero-sum games.
